questionText: A curve is defined by $x = \dfrac{1 - t^{2}}{1 + t^{2}}$ and $y = \dfrac{2t}{1 + t^{2}}$, where $t$ is any real number. Show that every point of the curve lies on the circle $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$, and determine the one point of that circle which the curve never reaches.

steps.workingLatex: \text{Compute } x^{2} + y^{2} \text{ and show it simplifies to } 1
steps.explanation: To prove the point lies on the circle $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$, the direct route is to add the squares of $x$ and $y$ and show the result is always $1$.
Squaring a fraction squares the top and the bottom separately; both share the denominator $(1 + t^{2})^{2}$, which will help when adding.

steps.description: Expand the numerator of x squared
steps.explanation: Expanding now makes the later addition of numerators straightforward.
Squaring $y$ gives the same denominator, so the two fractions can be combined directly.
Because the denominators match, we simply add the numerators over the shared denominator.
Combining the two $t^{2}$ terms, $-2t^{2} + 4t^{2} = 2t^{2}$, tidies the numerator into a recognisable form.
The numerator is exactly the square of $1 + t^{2}$, which is precisely the denominator; this is what makes the ratio collapse.
The identical numerator and denominator cancel, so $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$ for every real $t$, proving each point lies on the unit circle.
The relation $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$ is the circle of radius $1$ centred at the origin; the curve is (part of) this circle.

steps.description: Ask which circle point is unreachable
steps.explanation: A rational parametrisation like this typically misses one point of the circle; the natural candidate is where $x = -1$, so we test if any $t$ produces it.

steps.description: Set x equal to minus one
steps.explanation: To see whether $x = -1$ is ever reached, we solve the equation; multiplying up by the positive denominator is safe since $1 + t^{2} > 0$.
The $-t^{2}$ terms cancel on both sides, leaving the impossible statement $1 = -1$; hence no finite $t$ gives $x = -1$.
As $t$ grows without bound the point creeps towards $x = -1$ while $y$ tends to $0$, but it never actually arrives there.
So the single point of the unit circle that the curve never touches is $(-1, 0)$.

steps.description: Confirm all other points are covered
steps.workingLatex: t = 0 \Rightarrow (1,\, 0);\ \ \text{every other point is attained for some } t
steps.explanation: For instance $t = 0$ gives the opposite point $(1,0)$, and as $t$ runs through the reals the curve sweeps the rest of the circle, missing only $(-1,0)$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Since $x^{2} + y^{2} = \dfrac{(1 + t^{2})^{2}}{(1 + t^{2})^{2}} = 1$ for all real $t$, every point lies on the unit circle $x^{2} + y^{2} = 1$. The only point of that circle the curve never reaches is $(-1,\, 0)$, approached as $t \to \pm\infty$.
